content: Hello everyone, has anyone ever tried to enable Rust with CONFIG_PREEMPT_RT or use it in any real-time context? We would like to start exploring this in Ubuntu, but first I wanna make sure that we're not doing any duplicate work.
There was an announcement about a Rust Realtime Operating System some weeks ago.
I would love to help with this, RT is one of my interests.

Dirk Behme said:

There was an announcement about a Rust Realtime Operating System some weeks ago.

Thanks for the mention!

During building RROS, one problem we met was that Rust may alloc memory implicitly. This could cause latency in the real-time code path. It needs careful implementation to avoid such situations. Another problem is that sometimes it's hard to keep pure safe code and low latency at the same time when manipulating a list.
If you have further plans to build real-time apps with Rust in RROS, we are glad to offer help. :big_smile:

Hongyu Li said:

If you have further plans to build real-time apps with Rust in RROS, we are glad to offer help. :big_smile:

We don't have any precise plan for now, we were just considering if we could attempt to enable Rust in the Ubuntu realtime kernel and I'm not sure how much time / effort we can allocate on this (it's more a managerial decision than a technical decision at this stage) :)
But I'll keep you informed once I have more details on this

Andrea Righi said:

But I'll keep you informed once I have more details on this

Thanks a lot!

Hongyu Li said:

During building RROS, one problem we met was that Rust may alloc memory implicitly. This could cause latency in the real-time code path.

Do you mean concerns like .pushing to a vector automatically resizing? Or just having a tough time of tracking which functions or types may allocate ever

Trevor Gross said:

Hongyu Li said:

During building RROS, one problem we met was that Rust may alloc memory implicitly. This could cause latency in the real-time code path.

Do you mean concerns like .pushing to a vector automatically resizing? Or just having a tough time of tracking which functions or types may allocate ever

It's kinda similar to the first case. For us, this occurs when we're adding a node into an rbtree. In Linux, adding the node to the tree doesn't involve any memory allocation since the rb_node is already allocated within the structure. But in Rust, when we try to insert using try_insert, it calls try_allocate_node, which then attempts to allocate memory.

We've also encountered the latter scenario. We tackled it by introducing a flag that indicates whether we're operating in the real-time code path. Then, within the memory allocation function, we check the state of this flag.
